It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Between regulatory ﬁeld structuring and organizational roles: Intermediation in the ﬁeld of sustainable urban development Joel Bothello Department of Management, John Molson School of Business, Concordia University, Montreal, Canada Afshin Mehrpouya Department of Accounting and Management Control, HEC Paris, Jouy-en-Josas, France Abstract Recent contributions in the domains of governance and regulation elucidate the importance of rule-intermediation (RI), the role that organizations adopt to bridge actors with regulatory or “rulemaking” roles and those with target or “rule-taking” roles. Intermediation not only enables the diffusion and translation of regulatory norms, but also allows for the representation of different actors in policymaking arenas. While prior studies have explored the roles that such RIs adopt to facilitate their intermediation functions, we have yet to consider how ﬁeld-level structuring processes inﬂuence (and are inﬂuenced by) the various and changing roles adopted by RI. In this study we focus on the mutually constitutive relations between ﬁeld-level change processes and the evolving roles of RIs by studying the rise of the International Council for Local Environmental Ini- tiatives (ICLEI)/Local Governments for Sustainability, an RI serving as a bridge for sustainable urban development policies between the United Nations and local authorities. Using ICLEI as an illustrative case, we theorize four different processes of regulatory ﬁeld structuration: problematization, role specialization, marketization, and orchestrated decentralization. We dis- cuss their implications for RI roles in the ﬁeld and further theorize the changing dynamics of trickle-up intermediation pro- cesses as an RI gains power and inﬂuence. Keywords: governance, intermediation, rule-intermediary, sustainable development. 1. Introduction In the contemporary era, norms such as competition, democracy, transparency, and environmentalism have come to dominate policymaking and organizational governance (Drori et al. 2006; Mehrpouya & Djelic 2014; Bothello & Salles-Djelic 2017). In this new paradigm, actors, technologies, and knowledge in governance have rapidly prolif- erated, leading to the rise of regulatory ﬁelds for different public goods (Gulbrandsen 2008; Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009; Fransen 2011; Reinecke et al. 2012). The types of actors involved in these ﬁelds vary widely, from intergov- ernmental – and therefore, state-mandated – organizations, such as the United Nations (UN), the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), and the European Union (Abbott et al. 2015; Abbott 2014), to a fast expanding apparatus of transnational private regulators, ranging from multistakeholder initiatives to industry-backed and commercial entities (Bartley 2007). The agendas of these actors are equally diverse, yet are broadly structured under norms of scientization (or reliance on expertise), marketization (or reliance on market-based organizing principles), democratization (or reliance on stakeholder consultation processes and related rituals for input legitimacy), and moral rationalization (or reliance on the creation of normative regimes that nudge targets toward compliance) (Djelic & Sahlin-Andersson 2005; Gulbrandsen 2008; Djelic 2011b). However, the effectiveness of transnational “rules” is contingent upon a constellation of rule-intermediaries (RIs) that not only enable translation, adaptation, and travelling of rules, but also facilitate accountability and Correspondence: Joel Bothello, Department of Management, John Molson School of Business, Concordia University, 1450 Guy St., Montreal, QC H3H 0A1, Canada. Email: joel.bothello@concordia.ca Accepted for publication 27 July 2018. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Regulation & Governance (2018) doi:10.1111/rego.12215 Page 2: push for compliance by the regulatory targets (Levi-Faur & Jordana 2005; Bartley 2007; Djelic 2011b; Abbott et al. 2015). Along with their implementation and monitoring roles in performing these functions, intermediaries also frequently act as representatives in transnational arenas, where they aggregate and promote the interests of (sub)national actors (Levi-Faur & Starobin 2014). In other words, RIs operate between regulatory rulemakers (RMs) and targets, or rule-takers (RTs); they are central to organizing the mutually constituting “trickle-up” (RT à RI à RM) and “trickle-down” (from RM à RI à RT) feedback processes in transnational governance (Djelic 2006; Auld & Renckens 2017). While recent research on intermediation has made major advances toward elucidating the characteristics of RIs in regulatory ﬁelds (Gulbrandsen 2008; Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009; Reinecke et al. 2012; Abbott et al. 2017a, b), these studies have traditionally examined RI roles without considering the inﬂuence of broader ﬁeld-level pro- cesses. An exploration of how the former conditions the latter – and vice versa – would not only contribute to a process-based understanding of intermediary governance but would also facilitate theory development regarding organizational responses accompanying ﬁeld (re)structuration. In this article, we aim to contribute to a growing domain of research investigating how RIs play a role in deﬁning, expanding, and shaping transnational governance (Abbott et al. 2015, 2017b,c). Building upon this body of research, we explore the evolving trickle-up roles of an RI in an emergent and evolving ﬁeld (Djelic 2011a; Abbott et al. 2015; Mair et al. 2012; Levi-Faur & Starobin 2014). Speciﬁcally, we describe how rulemaking devel- opments alter the roles and functions of intermediaries, speciﬁcally highlighting the role transformations of one speciﬁc intermediary as it responds to such changes. Taking the International Council for Local Environmental Initiatives (ICLEI)/Local Governments for Sustainability as a case study, our paper investigates the conditions under which this RI emerged, identifying how its trickle-up roles shifted over time as a response to the structur- ing of the ﬁeld of sustainable urban development. ICLEI is a local government organization (LGO)1 (Brugmann 2007) that was established in 1990 to represent local authorities at transnational fora related to environmentalism. The organization offers an exemplar case for examining the interplay between role changes and the trickle-up processes of rulemaking: it is the longest running and largest urban sustainability LGO with approximately 1,200 local members in 43 countries, employing more than 200 staff. ICLEI has been active in driving numerous “ﬁeld conﬁguring events” (Lampel & Meyer 2008), promoting local participation in global regulation since the emergence of the sustainable urban development ﬁeld in the late 1980s. The organization was notably central to the establishment of Local Agenda 21 (LA21), a land- mark sustainable urban development program engaging more than 6,400 cities in towns in 113 countries (Brugmann 2007). Given the co-evolution of sustainable urban development practices and ICLEI, the organiza- tion offers a compelling case for examining the mutually constitutive interactions between regulatory intermedi- aries and the ﬁeld (Lafferty & Eckerberg 2013; Leffel, 2017; Kaufman & Zernike 2012). Through our study of the ﬁeld of sustainable urban development and of ICLEI, we aim to make two contribu- tions to studies of regulatory intermediation. Our ﬁrst contribution is to highlight the value of simultaneous ana- lyses of ﬁeld-level structures and RIs’ embedded agency (Emirbayer & Mische 1998). We show how the ﬁeld of sustainable urban development evolves through four ﬁeld-structuring processes: problematization, role specializa- tion, marketization, and orchestrated decentralization. As ICLEI’s power and legitimacy subsequently expand – and as the regulatory ﬁeld evolves – its relations to both RTs and RMs change. The organization shifts from direct work in representation to more facilitative roles, including curating and orchestrating practices. In explicat- ing these changes, our article shows how the shifting relational roles between regulatory actors theorized about in prior literature (Havinga & Verbruggen 2017) may in fact be organizational responses to ﬁeld-level dynamics. Our second contribution is to provide new insights into the dynamics of trickle-up processes, speciﬁcally the regulatory intermediary actions aiming to intervene in rulemaking. In the introduction to this special issue, the authors emphasize the importance of attending to the level of ofﬁcialization and formalization of RIs in their mutually constitutive relations to RMs and targets (Mena et al. 2018). Using ICLEI as an example, we show how an RI may begin with a representation mandate from certain RTs (local authorities) that contests the role of established representatives (federal governments). This RI may then increasingly adopt expanded ofﬁcial roles but also a set of “unofﬁcial” roles in shaping ﬁeld (sustainable urban development) rules to allow the participation of local actors. We emphasize the role of ﬁeld-level changes in these “mandate shifts” at this RI. We identify four distinct RI trickle-up processes: “insurgent,” “technocratic,” “curator,” and “orchestrator” trickle-up © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 2 J. Bothello and A. Mehrpouya Field structuring and intermediary roles Page 3: intermediation, demonstrating how these four intermediation processes are built on fundamentally different rela- tions to RMs, RTs, and other RIs. Our paper is organized as follows. We begin by outlining recent research on regulatory intermediation, specif- ically highlighting the importance of intermediary roles in the process. Building upon prior research, we illustrate that intermediation roles are ﬂuid and change according to the evolution of the regulatory ﬁeld. We describe our research design and methodology, motivating our selection of ICLEI as a case study of interest. We then present our ﬁndings, organized according to speciﬁc phases in the regulatory ﬁeld of sustainable urban development, link- ing these phases to role transformations. We conclude the paper with a discussion of how the ﬁndings contribute to research on intermediation. 2. Regulatory intermediation: Between ﬁeld and roles In recent decades, transnational governance regimes have shifted from state and intergovernmental regulation to a diverse set of private regulatory actors that compete to establish global “rules of the game” (Djelic & Sahlin- Andersson 2006). The accompanying explosion in the number and variety of actors, knowledge forms, and tech- nologies currently driving regulation has been termed as a shift toward “regulatory capitalism” (Levi-Faur & Jor- dana 2005). Although this phenomenon has traditionally been examined through dualist analyses of RMs and RTs, the sophistication, expansion, and diversity of contemporary regulatory ecosystems require novel conceptual tools (Abbott et al. 2017a). Accordingly, recent political science literature has begun attending to this complexity by highlighting the role of “regulatory intermediaries;” in this model, regulatory roles are broken down into “reg- ulator” or “RM,” “RI,” and “target” or “RT” roles. Levi-Faur and Starobin broadly deﬁne RIs as “regulatory actors with the capacity to affect, control, and monitor relations between rule-makers and rule-takers via their interpre- tations of standards and their role in the increasingly institutionalized processes of monitoring, veriﬁcation, test- ing, auditing, and certiﬁcation” (2014, p. 21). This literature has conceptualized different reasons for regulators to rely on regulatory intermediaries, which include: operational capacity, legitimacy, and the relative independence of intermediaries compared with regulators (Abbott et al. 2017a). In more recent work, however, RM, RT, and RI positions are conceptualized less as ﬁxed, organization types and more as contingent, relational roles for regulatory actors. Havinga and Verbruggen (2017), for example, show how one organization can be “chameleonic,” with roles that vary according to the type of stakeholder and timing. These advancements elucidate the need for more constructivist studies of RIs, with more attention paid to the role of discourses and knowledge in the workings of RIs (Mena et al. 2018). What has been missing, though, is an examination of how ﬁeld-level structural dynamics inﬂuence the type of roles adopted by a RI. Prior work often assumes that role adoption and changes are “actor-centric,” the result of allocation by RMs (Abbott et al. 2015), the welfare of RTs (Koenig-Archibugi & Macdonald 2017), or agentic action on the part of intermediaries themselves (Van der Heijden 2017). In other words, attention to contextual inﬂuences (e.g. ﬁeld structures and processes) on RI role dynamics has been scarce. Several papers in this area have hinted at how regulatory ﬁeld2 emergence and stabilization can affect the type of intermediation that occurs. Reinecke et al. (2012) have illustrated how marketization emerged in the private regulation of fair trade food when organizations reached dynamic equilibrium between competition and coopera- tion. Similarly, in his analysis of the regulatory ﬁeld for working conditions in garment manufacturing, Fransen (2011) revealed the conditions for the rise of a competition dynamic, and how different private regulators repre- sent disparate political coalitions with different deﬁnitions of the problem and the solution, preventing consolida- tion among seven private regulators. Dingwerth and Pattberg (2009) focused instead on the role of institutional norms, their institutionalization, and their relations with neighboring ﬁelds in bringing stability to regulatory ﬁelds. These studies point to the impact of ﬁeld consolidation and/or fragmentation on regulatory actors roles, yet have not explicitly examined their mutually constitutive interactions. We attend to this area in our article. The regulatory ﬁeld we focus on provides an example of a ﬁeld that has undergone rapid consolidation during the past 20 years. The actor at the center of our analysis, ICLEI, played a central role both in the emergence and maintenance of the “local sustainable development” regulatory ﬁeld. We focus on the changing roles of ICLEI and their relations to the changing structure of the regulatory ﬁeld. Research design, case selection and methods 3.1. Research setting In examining how role changes of a focal intermediary occur, we elected to study ICLEI, an intermediary estab- lished in 1990 to promote local interests in the formulation of sustainable urban development principles. We spe- ciﬁcally chose this organization for two reasons: First, ICLEI acts in a dual capacity, as an “information clearinghouse” providing sustainability support for subnational governments, but also as a representative for local governments in international fora. It thus offered an ideal illustrative case (Flyvbjerg 2006; Siggelkow 2007) for examining role changes and trickle-up and trickle-down processes involved in rulemaking and mediation. Sec- ondly, ICLEI has been identiﬁed as a key actor in the diffusion of sustainable urban development practices (Lafferty & Eckerberg 2013; Kaufman & Zernike 2012) and has been the largest environmental city network in the world for two decades (Leffel, 2017). If the success of an intermediary’s initiatives is deﬁned in terms of membership and longevity, then the ICLEI offers an exemplar case – the organization is the oldest urban sustainability intermediary currently operating, with approximately 1,200 local members in 43 countries, employing more than 200 staff. 3.2. Research design We use a case study research design to examine how the establishment and roles of a focal RI can be inﬂuenced by changes in the regulatory ﬁeld (Flyvbjerg 2006; Siggelkow 2007; Yin 2013). Following a process-thinking approach (Langley 1999), we investigate how certain organizational outcomes and changes can be theorized as chronologically sequential responses to external developments. In our speciﬁc case, the analysis is based on iden- tifying the salient processes and major developments in the regulatory ﬁeld (as revealed within ofﬁcial texts and RM reports) and examining reactions by intermediaries (e.g. appropriation, elaboration, deﬂection, rejection). This model of analysis thus uses macro patterns to explain the micro processes (i.e. role shifts) evident in organi- zation (Ventresca & Mohr 2002, p. 813). However, our intermediary of interest is novel as this process also works in reverse: role adoption affects ﬁeld structuration, as well as the roles adopted by proximate actors. The micro-processes involved in role transforma- tion thus have an impact on ﬁeld-level processes. We accordingly investigated how texts and preparatory docu- ments created by ICLEI (and other intermediaries) were adopted and formalized within RM declarations, conventions, agreements, and reports. We employed a historiographic method of discourse analysis, utilizing organizational archives as source mate- rial (Ventresca & Mohr 2002). Unlike nomothetic approaches that treat archives as datasets, historiographies are idiographic in nature, favoring in-depth scrutiny of discourse produced by a focal organization. A detailed and disciplined reading of myriad – and often unstandardized – sources permits the development of a “thicker” com- prehension of an object of inquiry (Geertz 1983). The sources of data are primarily comprised of (bi)annual reports and newsletters over a span of two decades, supplemented by organizational charters, planning/program guides, internal memos and correspondence, archived web pages, and academic articles and book chapters written by organizational members over the same period. Our corpus of texts was thus exhaustive in terms of publicly released documentation. Given that mediation is a relational activity, we also incorporated discourse produced by stakeholders with salient ties to the focal organization, focusing on the prominent organizations adopting rule- making and intermediation roles within the same regulatory ﬁeld. For the RMs, documents from major UN fora and preparatory committees (PrepComs) were crucial for reconstructing the discourse that enabled ﬁeld-level changes, as well as identifying the relevant actors populating this ﬁeld. In parallel, we sought to situate the role changes vis-à-vis other RIs in the regulatory ﬁeld. The aim was to analyze the tripartite inﬂuences among the focal intermediary, the ﬁeld, and other RIs. A list of the archival sources is provided in Appendix I, totaling approximately 1,300 pages of data. As a source of triangulation, we complemented our historiography with a set of six semi-structured interviews that could provide corroboration and further insights. We interviewed two founding members of the focal organi- zation, as well as major executives involved in leadership positions since the organization was established in 1990. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 4 J. Sustainable urban development as a regulatory ﬁeld The emergence of sustainable urban development as a regulatory ﬁeld can be traced to the 1987 launch of a land- mark environmental report titled Our Common Future (also known as the Brundtland Report) issued by the UN World Commission on Environment and Development (Bulkeley & Betsill 2005; Bothello & Salles-Djelic 2017). The Report was the ﬁrst to situate sustainable urban development on equal footing with other regulatory ﬁelds, such as food security, ecosystem protection, and population growth (World Commission on Environment and Development [WCED] 1987). The “Urban Challenge” cast cities as drivers of – but also solutions to – environ- mental problems, a development further reinforced by the 1992 publication of a more detailed UN program named Local Agenda 21 (LA21) that acted as a planning guide for urban sustainability (International Council for Local Environmental Initiatives [ICLEI], International Development Research Centre [IDRC], United Nations Environment Programme [UNEP] 1996). In subsequent decades, a variety of regulatory instruments, both bind- ing and non-binding, were launched: the United Kingdom (UK), for example, established statutory performance standards in 2000 for municipalities regarding air quality and waste directly based on LA21 (Bulkeley & Betsill 2005). Similarly, by 2007 all Swedish municipalities had adopted LA21 policies (Eckerberg & Forsberg 1998). Beyond the nation-state, various transnational organizations crafted comprehensive non-binding programs, such as “2030” or “Gold Standard” initiatives to promote long-term development of sustainable cities (United Cities and Local Governments [UCLG] 2010; Climate-KIC 2017), accompanying more speciﬁc technical standards, such as Leadership in Energy and Environment Design (LEED) and more recently ISO 37000 indicators for the sus- tainable development of communities. Accordingly, we conceptualize sustainable urban development as a regulatory ﬁeld in its own right. Despite some overlap with proximate regulatory ﬁelds, such as population growth and poverty alleviation, sustainable urban development has become populated by a diverse set of specialized public and private actors. In concert, the ﬁeld also contains speciﬁc programs, policies, norms, standards, certiﬁcates, and rankings designed to direct local authorities toward collectively agreed-upon outcomes. The pressure for RTs to conform to these urban sustain- ability principles can be coercive, through the threat of legal sanctions, as illustrated by mandatory state policies, but also through the process of “naming and shaming” (Boli 2006), where legitimacy is contingent upon adoption (Guler et al. 2002). In Figure 1, we outline the evolution of the ﬁeld over the course of two decades, indicating the relationships between and among intermediaries during this time and the role responses of ICLEI. 5. Field Phase #1 (1986–1990): Problematization 5.1. Sustainable urban development as a response to environmental tragedies of the commons The Brundtland Report was the ﬁrst major environmental report to highlight the importance of effective urban governance for sustainable development: by predicting that “half of humanity [would] live in cities” (WCED 1987, p. 22) by the 21st century, the Report positioned “The Urban Challenge” as an issue interlocked with other “tragedies of the commons” (Hardin 1968), such as overpopulation, poverty, and natural resource consumption (United Nations General Assembly [UNGA] 1983). In practical terms, the Report called for the empowerment of local authorities through decentralization and delegation of political, institutional, and ﬁnancial resources, as well as through strengthening UN leadership on human settlements through programs such as UN-Habitat (WCED 1987, p. 214). Under this broad canopy of issues and mechanisms, the Brundtland Report triggered a series of public and private initiatives to address economic, social, and environmental issues (Bothello & Salles-Djelic 2017). © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 5 Field structuring and intermediary roles J. Bothello and A. Mehrpouya Page 6: This push was supplemented by another ﬁeld development in the same year. In 1987, the UN ratiﬁed the Montreal Protocol, a multilateral agreement designed to encourage phase-outs of ozone depleting substances. The Protocol originated from research in the late 1970s demonstrating the harmful effects of chloroﬂuorocarbons (CFCs) on the stratosphere. Notably, the protocol was the ﬁrst environmental accord to be ratiﬁed by all mem- bers: proponents of the Protocol successfully framed ozone depletion as a particularly insidious tragedy of the commons that would have detrimental impacts on every constituent country. Cited by UN ofﬁcials as “perhaps the single most successful international agreement to date,” the Protocol was pivotal in highlighting the urgency of environmental issues (United Nations [UN] 2003). Together, the ratiﬁcation of the Montreal Protocol and the publication of the Brundtland Report opened a new policymaking role of cities: sustainable urban development became a necessary component for addressing a host of time-sensitive environmental issues (WCED 1987, p. 34). The need for cities to handle ozone depletion was particularly acute: the city of Irvine, California with a population of only 100,000 people was alone responsi- ble for 0.25 percent of global chloroﬂuorocarbon (CFC) production. In addition, the Brundtland Report also spec- iﬁed the means for addressing these issues, through empowered local authorities as opposed to state-sponsored policies alone. In this manner, the ﬁeld of sustainable urban development would feature some regulatory overlap with proximate ﬁelds but would also feature actors and initiatives distinct to the ﬁeld itself. The problematization of cities as core to the ozone depletion emergency – as well as the projection of urban governance as a crucial pil- lar of the solution – proved instrumental to the emergence of the sustainable urban development regulatory ﬁeld. 5.2. Rule-intermediary dynamics: The “Trickle-up Theory of Public Policy” In the years preceding the Montreal Protocol and the Brundtland Report, a movement for local self-governance had begun in the US, spearheaded by an association named the Center for Innovative Diplomacy (CID). The association was formed in the early 1980s, founded on the belief “that local elected ofﬁcials had the legitimacy, knowledge and the temperament to self-problem solve on a global scale.”3 In concert with the National League of Cities – the largest national association of American municipalities – the CID mobilized local authorities to pass resolutions on regulatory issues around a nuclear freeze and divestment from apartheid South Africa (Center for Innovative Diplomacy 1986). By circumventing federal policy though, these cities were branded by the federal government as “renegade local governments” (Agran 1989). A sprinkling of cities, and then more, copy them, until, at last, the federal government itself [sees] the wis- dom of the new thinking” (Agran 1989, p. 4). In this “trickle-up theory of public policy,” the CID adopted a role as a diffuser of “common-sense policies” among constituents. The CID rapidly capitalized upon political momentum around sustainable development and ozone depletion as launching pads to push toward urban self-governance. Shortly after the Montreal Protocol was agreed, the association channeled the efforts of members to develop programs aimed at reducing CFCs: The signatory nations of the 1987 Montreal Protocol… now appear ready to phase out CFCs totally by cen- tury’s end… But there is little chance that Washington, or Bonn or London or Moscow, will act as rapidly and decisively as many localities are ready to act. Considering that the ozone is breaking up much more quickly than many scientists or policy-makers anticipated… local ofﬁcials are showing more prudence than impatience in acting on their own. (Sklansky 1989, p. 10) The CID thus framed CFC phase-outs as effective through local action, the urgency of which justiﬁed bypass- ing national governments. The ﬁrst city to do so was Irvine in July 1989 (Bailey 1989), followed by 30 other American municipalities (Labaeye 2010). The CID catalyzed this wave of CFC phase-outs, organizing a Strato- spheric Protection Accord (SPA) conference in Irvine a few days after the law passed, attended by representatives from two dozen municipalities (Sklansky 1989). One organizer of the SPA noted: “[CID members] organized Canadian and American cities for the SPA, a kind of edgy declaration. They could have just called it an agree- ment but they said, ‘A treaty on protecting the stratosphere.’”4 Following the SPA, all attendant municipalities pledged to pass CFC phase-outs. 5.3. Role emergence: “Insurgent” trickle-up intermediation By organizing the SPA, the CID orchestrated cities to collectively act in a rulemaking capacity, a process meant to facilitate involvement in global policymaking. Despite (or because of ) this success, the CID mission of self-governance and trickle-up policymaking became controversial among various stakeholder groups. Federal ofﬁcials reprimanded the CID for interfering with foreign policy, while sister city organizations and conservative local ofﬁcials became concerned about progressive politics being introduced into their work.5 In response, CID moved to support the creation of a distinct intermediary that could combine environmental- ism and local self-governance, using the SPA as an anchoring event. Without promoting their afﬁliation, CID executives garnered support from constituent municipalities – as well as an endorsement from the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) – for a “World Congress of Local Governments for a Sus- tainable Future” held at the UN, with the participation of 200 local governments from 43 countries (ICLEI 1993). CID was involved only in a support capacity, as “an NGO [platform] for raising money.”6 At the con- gress, a charter was ratiﬁed for the creation of ICLEI, with the organization beginning operations in 1991. In a clear demonstration of the boundary-spanning nature of this intermediary, the ﬁrst chairman noted that the mission of ICLEI was to “provide[s] the local government community with new capacity for strengthen- ing democratic local governance… in the face of major challenges of environment and development” (ICLEI 1993, p. 3). The creation of ICLEI – along with the sequence of events preceding it – reveals how a nascent regulatory ﬁeld can occasion role formation by an intermediary (Meyer & Höllerer 2010). Sustainable urban development was a subset of an emerging ﬁeld of sustainable development, although it became a ﬁeld in its own right through a process of cooptation by proponents of local self-governance. The role played by ICLEI during this phase was one of “insurgent trickle-up,” where the agendas of local governments were aggregated into one representative intermediary, tasked with directing inﬂuence on global sustainable development politics and bypassing federal apparatuses. This phase of ICLEI’s history illustrates how, when boundaries of a new ﬁeld are ill deﬁned and the nature of the issue itself is being formed, an intermediary’s role can also be constructed based on the agenda and intervention of intermediaries in proximate ﬁelds. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 7 Field structuring and intermediary roles J.
