(Local) Agenda 21 In 1992, the UN held a Conference on Environment and Development (UNCED) – commonly known as the Earth Summit – in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil to build upon the foundation established in the Brundtland Report (WCED 1987; UN Conference on Environment and Development [UNCED] 1992). Aside from adopting a bind- ing Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC), UN members also agreed to a set of voluntary and nonbinding environmental principles named Agenda 21, ratiﬁed by 178 heads of state. Agenda 21 was pivotal as it was the ﬁrst environmental report to specify roles for different actors: notably, it included a chapter entrench- ing the “participation and cooperation of local authorities [as] a determining factor in fulﬁlling [Agenda 21] objectives” (UNCED 1992). This “Local Agenda 21” (or LA21) proposed objectives toward improving informa- tion exchange between local governments, as well as partnerships among “relevant organs and organizations such as UNDP, UNCHS, UNEP, the World Bank, regional banks, IULA [etc.]” (UNCED 1992). In addition, the Report also included a chapter deﬁning a role for the scientiﬁc and technological community in decisionmaking on the environment and development (UNCED 1992, p. 31.1). As such, the Earth Summit represented a critical juncture in the ﬁeld of local sustainable development: multiple stakeholders agreed upon the issues and their scope, with an emphasis on division of tasks and speciﬁc roles. Agenda 21, in concert with the lengthy prepara- tory processes for the conference, served to catalyze a ﬁeld shift away from problematization and toward specialization. 6.2. Rule-intermediary dynamics: Political divergence At the time of the Earth Summit, the two largest international associations representing local authorities were the International Union of Local Authorities (IULA) and United Towns Organization (UTO). Despite having special consultancy status at major UN events (Bislev & Salskov-Iversen 2001), both entities had been absent from prior meetings on sustainable development. Nonetheless, they shared similar goals of facilitating interurban coopera- tion and promoting local self-governance, paralleling the role of the CID (by now, defunct). However, the two organizations were historically divided along Cold War ideological lines: IULA included North American and Western European municipalities and advocacy groups, while UTO membership was comprised primarily of socialist French municipalities and former Eastern Bloc cities. At UN events these divergent political views cre- ated friction regarding who truly represented local government. As one Earth Summit participant noted of this “competing and positioning” dynamic (Hom 2002, p. 254): IULA and UTO were duking it out over who was going to be the main voice of local government in a post- Cold War era… at the prepcoms for the Earth Summit, mayors from the two organizations would have a photo-op with the UN Secretary General, and then have side meetings and bicker with one another… This [conﬂict] made it very difﬁcult to hold meetings.7 In parallel to the Earth Summit, thousands of civil society representatives participated in a Global Forum drafting 40 “alternative treaties.” Non-governmental organizations (NGOs) were frustrated that they were rele- gated to consultative or observer status rather than viable implementers of sustainable development policy. One treaty, drafted by an NGO called Habitat International Coalition (HIC), was labeled “Towards Just Democratic and Sustainable Cities, Towns and Villages” and featured language mirroring LA21. The treaty was signed by 140 NGOs. As an Earth Summit participant from ICLEI noted: [Al]though they weren’t able to sit in the working group meetings like we were – to negotiate and introduce language – I think the [NGOs] legitimately had the ear of people in the Secretariat and of certain govern- ments, saying “you can’t let these [local associations] kidnap our agenda.”8 In this manner, the regulatory ﬁeld of local sustainable development leading up to the Earth Summit was fragmented with competing actors and agendas. Collectively they were struggling to formalize their role in rule- making arenas in competition with national governments. However, given the immature and unstructured state of this emerging ﬁeld, the key actors were divided along their political agendas and the roles of actors (such as ICLEI, IULA, UTO) were ill-deﬁned, contested, and overlapped. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 8 J. Bothello and A. Mehrpouya Field structuring and intermediary roles Page 9: 6.3. Role adoption: “Technocratic” trickle-up intermediation The overarching ﬁeld of sustainable development emerged, in part, through the Montreal Protocol and the fram- ing of issues like ozone depletion as scientiﬁc rather than political problems. Politically aligned organizations like IULA and UTO faced resistance at the UN from the US and China who viewed local governance advocacy as potentially subversive to state authority (Brugmann 1990). ICLEI, on the other hand, adopted a technocratic approach better suited to address a ﬁeld increasingly oriented toward technical solutions, making local gover- nance more politically palatable. For instance, the organization emphasized technology for energy efﬁciency or design or streets, rather than policies of investment or procurement.9 In other words, by “rendering technical” (Li 2011) the environmental issues, ICLEI succeeded in maintaining a less contested presence in the ﬁeld com- pared to counterpart associations: “While IULA and UTO were duking it out… [We were] sitting at a desk with a little sign saying ‘Local Authorities’ working out relationships with different government delegations, introduc- ing language into this chapter or that chapter.”10 This “technocratic” role allowed ICLEI to maneuver around the politics historically associated with local governance and engage in the drafting of LA21 and related chapters. ICLEI members involved in the drafting of LA21 removed the name of ICLEI from the text, instead crediting the chapter to IULA and UTO (Hom 2002): In the end, the thing that had to be sacriﬁced so that [IULA and UTO] would not sabotage Local Agenda 21 was the name of ICLEI… there’s this irony that ICLEI launched and led it worldwide, and came up with the concept as well-recognized, but our name isn’t mentioned in it. It was a concession to them, you know.11 By adding both IULA and UTO as authors on LA21, ICLEI secured the endorsement of both associations, adding member localities from both Western and former Soviet countries (ICLEI 1993, 1996). However, given that neither association had any capabilities or expertise in sustainable development, ICLEI would become the default intermediary for assisting local governments in implementing LA21 (ICLEI et al. 1996). Following the Summit, ICLEI launched a core program for LA21 implementation, followed by numerous initiatives oriented toward technical support, training, and consulting on local environmental issues. Through these programs, 1,800 cities and towns adopted LA21 between 1992 and 1996, with ICLEI membership growing from 30 localities to 186 within the same timeframe (ICLEI 1996). As the secretary general of UNEP noted of ICLEI’s involvement: “the leadership of the International Council for Local Environmental Initiatives… [on] global–local interaction is one of the most encouraging and promising developments to have occurred since and as a result of the Earth Summit… (Strong, 1997)” This phase in ICLEI’s history demonstrates how a process of ﬁeld fragmentation can occur even in a relatively nascent ﬁeld: actors with ostensible intermediation capacity enter the ﬁeld and import their ideological divides into regulatory and governance debates. However, this fragmentation – combined with the specialization and scientization of the ﬁeld – can also condition a transformation for the focal intermediary: ICLEI managed to complement the emphasis on local governance with a more technical role afforded by the growing prominence of ozone depletion. In doing so, the LGO managed to bypass the ideological conﬂicts and contestation of partner organizations and construct a coalition along seemingly uncontroversial technical capacity. In this way, the role adopted by the organization represents a pressure for consolidation in sustainable urban development – at least along technical concerns and implementation processes. 7. Field Phase #3 (1994–1997): Marketization 7.1. The Aalborg Charter and Habitat II In the mid-1990s, two major international events were organized around local governance and sustainable devel- opment. The ﬁrst was the 1994 European Conference on Sustainable Cities and Towns, a major gathering on urban sustainability held in Aalborg, Denmark and co-organized by the European Commission and the Danish government. The major outcome of this congress was the “Aalborg Charter,” a reiteration of LA21 goals that included the necessity of self-governance to achieve these goals (City of Aalborg, ICLEI 1994). The event was the largest sustainable development meeting held in Europe up to that point; within a decade 1,600 municipalities © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 9 Field structuring and intermediary roles J. Bothello and A. Mehrpouya Page 10: adopted the Charter (and by extension, LA21) (Bulkeley et al. 2003). Aalborg thus represented an important juncture where LA21 became entrenched as the dominant norm in the ﬁeld of sustainable urban development. A second pivotal event was the 1996 UN-Habitat II Conference in Istanbul, a summit on human development following up from the Habitat I Conference two decades earlier. As at Rio in 1992, participants reiterated support of LA21 without references to self-governance or urban associations to avoid antagonizing the US and China (UN-Habitat 1996). However, the United Nations Human Settlements Programme (UNCHS) also pushed during the preparatory processes for a program to compete with LA21: One of the tussles in 1996 was that Habitat wanted to have something like Local Agenda 21, a big popular global program, so they pushed something called “Local Habitat Agendas” … So every time they read the lan- guage we put in, the next day the text would come back and, lo and behold, there was nothing about LA21.12 “Local Habitat Agendas” nonetheless failed to appear within the Habitat Agenda report; UN members includ- ing Sweden and the UK voted against the initiative, as they had already committed to a broad implementation of LA21 in Aalborg. In subsequent years, however, the Agenda produced a Global Urban Observatory (GUO) allow- ing “comparative international evaluation of progress in meeting the aims of the Habitat Agenda” (United Nations Human Settlements Programme [UNCHS] 1997, p. 7). Speciﬁcally, this included a program of 46 key urban indicators, designed to “monitor overall urban performance” (UNCHS 1997). The events in Istanbul represent a process of marketization in the regulatory ﬁeld (Reinecke et al. 2012), where actors produce distinct standards to compete with the dominant incumbent. Local Habitat Agenda repre- sents a framework that competes with LA21 for legitimacy, one that is replete with programs for ranking and comparing RTs along certain criteria. In one sense, then, the emergence of these competing frameworks was a development that created competition among intermediaries in the regulatory ﬁeld, reﬂecting a broader competi- tion between different political coalitions. As Fransen (2011) has shown, conﬂicting agendas in these settings is structured in the guise of competing governance solutions leading to sustained fragmentation of the ﬁeld. 7.2. Rule-intermediary dynamics: Consolidated competition Paradoxically, although the process of marketization resulted in standards fragmentation, the resulting competi- tive pressures served to consolidate actors in competing coalitions within the ﬁeld. IULA and UTO were absent from the formal preparatory committees of both the Aalborg conference and Habitat II (City of Aalborg, ICLEI 1994; UN-Habitat 1996), as they were “largely disoriented about the ins-and-outs, the politics, and the emerging diplomacy of local government in [these events].”13 Instead, on the eve of the Habitat summit, they joined a par- allel assembly held by local government associations to discuss the formation of a new intermediary association, one that could serve as a collective representative for constituent associations at UN fora (UNCHS and World Associations of Cities and Local Authorities Coordination [WACLAC] 2005). ICLEI, as a participant in the main conference, was not involved in the discussions. The assembly declaration gave rise to a new umbrella intermedi- ary named the World Associations of Cities and Local Authorities Coordination (WACLAC), aggregated from IULA, UTO, and continental urban associations (Alger 2003, p. 99; Smith 2013). In its early years, the major goal of the organization was to create a Charter of Local Self-Government, an “internationally agreed, adaptable framework for the practice of local democracy…. To contribute to sustainable human and settlement develop- ment” (UNCHS and WACLAC 2005, p. 2). This focus on self-governance and the environment – along with the WACLAC’s role as liaison with UNCHS – meant that the new organization would be competing directly with ICLEI as the “voice” of local governments at UN fora. In addition, WACLAC also encouraged the use of the competing Global Urban Observatory (GUO) program devised by UNCHS (UN-Habitat 1998, p. 13). 7.3. Role adoption: “Curator” trickle-up intermediation Despite its early technocratic role, by 1996 ICLEI was openly critical regarding the use of GUO sustainability indicators and chose to withdraw from involvement in Habitat-II – this, despite the LGO being the only non- state member granted a participatory status equal to that of G77 members. The Secretary-General of ICLEI cited the failure of such indicators in Seattle (a Habitat II award winner but not an ICLEI member) while underscoring © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 10 J. As stated by an informant: How do you develop a relatively standardized methodology for cities to do greenhouse gas accounting? Are you going to be ultra-scientiﬁc, are you going for comparability, or are you going to provide people with the information they need to change their outcomes locally? ICLEI was always biased towards the latter.14 ICLEI subsequently shifted away from its role as a technocratic organization involved in policymaking, instead becoming a “curator” of environmental best practices of member municipalities. The organization main- tained the mandate of ofﬁcial representative of municipalities in global governance arenas, but this was comple- mented by the coordination of sustainable environmental policies and practices by those municipalities. New programs focused on this emerging intermediary role included an ICLEI Consultants program and ICLEI case studies designed to improve environmental performance (ICLEI 1996, 2000; Bulkeley 2006), while the European Secretariat drafted a 12-volume manual on good environmental practices (ICLEI 1996). This curating role was also facilitated by regulatory developments: Following the failure of the competing Local Habitat Agendas and frustrated by a lack of progress on the WACLAC Charter of Self-Governance, UN-Habitat opted to partner with ICLEI to launch a counterpart to the GUO program labeled The Best Practices and Local Leadership Program (BLP). By 1996, the regulatory ﬁeld around sustainable urban development had become more competitive with the emergence of a competing standard (Local Habitat Agendas), as well as a powerful rival intermediary (WACLAC), the latter of which was powerful enough to attract and aggregate numerous other intermediaries under its umbrella. Partially as a result of this marketization and contestation dynamic – with the challenger adopting a similar technocratic role as the incumbent – ICLEI responded by shifting away from the more techni- cal aspects of sustainable urban development. Instead, the LGO replaced this role with a “curator” role that was complementary to that of local governance representation and could leverage the activities of an established membership base.
